critic_raw:
The central design problem is using gradient-boosted trees versus logistic regression as the model comparison when the hypothesis is about whether SDOH features improve prediction. These are two different questions conflated into one experiment. If the GBT model with SDOH outperforms logistic regression with clinical features alone, the observed improvement could be due to (a) SDOH features, (b) the superior model class, or (c) the interaction of both. The appropriate experimental design for testing the SDOH hypothesis would hold the model class constant — e.g., compare GBT+SDOH vs. GBT-only vs. logistic regression with SDOH — so the feature contribution is separable from the architectural contribution.

Second, SDOH features like income, education, and housing status are missing not at random in EHR data. Patients from underserved communities — precisely those for whom SDOH is most predictive of readmission — are more likely to have incomplete SDOH records. The design mentions imputation on the training set but does not address the informative missingness structure: the pattern of which fields are missing is itself predictive and may be more informative than the imputed values. Without modeling or at minimum analyzing the missingness mechanism, the SDOH feature contribution will be underestimated.

Third, the stratified random split over five years of EHR data ignores temporal distribution shift in clinical practice. Hospital readmission prevention programs, policy changes (e.g., CMS penalty programs), and coding practice updates all change the relationship between clinical features and readmission rates over time. A random split allows the model to train on more-recent (post-policy) records and test on older ones, which inflates generalization performance relative to prospective deployment.

Fourth, AUC-ROC is the primary metric, but for a clinical decision support tool, the relevant operating point is determined by resource constraints (number of care coordinators, outreach capacity). AUC-ROC summarizes performance across all thresholds equally, including thresholds that are operationally infeasible. The design should report calibration and performance at a fixed specificity or at the operational outreach quota — AUC alone does not support deployment decisions.
defender_raw:
The stratified random split is relatively defensible for hospital readmission prediction, though not unimpeachable. The 30-day readmission outcome for a given patient depends primarily on that patient's clinical state at discharge, comorbidities, and social circumstances — all cross-sectional signals. While hospital practices change over time and coding practices evolve, a five-year EHR dataset with stratified sampling does capture temporal variety. The argument that temporal autocorrelation is weak enough to justify random splitting is not unreasonable here, though a chronological split sensitivity analysis would strengthen the design.

The hypothesis being tested — does adding SDOH features improve prediction? — is tested through a within-algorithm comparison: gradient-boosted trees with clinical-only features vs. gradient-boosted trees with clinical plus SDOH features. This is the right ablation design for an incremental feature value question. However, the design as described compares gradient-boosted trees against logistic regression, not gradient-boosted trees against gradient-boosted trees with a feature subset. This conflates model architecture with feature set, making it impossible to attribute performance differences to SDOH features specifically versus algorithm expressiveness.

AUC-ROC as primary metric is appropriate for a readmission risk ranking task and standard in this literature. The stratified demographic subgroup analysis is a genuine strength — it ensures observed improvements are not artifacts of demographic confounding.

The SDOH feature sourcing (income, education, housing status) from EHRs is mentioned without specifying missingness rates, which are typically severe for SDOH in electronic records. This is a real gap: if SDOH features are missing for 60% of patients, the comparison tests something different than the hypothesis claims.
all_issues_raised: Confounding the SDOH feature contribution with the model class difference (GBT vs. logistic regression) makes it impossible to attribute observed improvements to SDOH features specifically; a within-model-class feature ablation is required.

SDOH features have informative missingness — patients from underserved communities are most likely to have missing SDOH records, meaning simple imputation systematically underestimates the SDOH signal where it matters most.

Stratified random split over five years of clinical data ignores policy-driven distribution shifts (CMS readmission penalties, coding practice changes) that alter the feature-label relationship over time.
AUC-ROC as the primary metric ignores the operational threshold at which the intervention is deployed; calibration and performance at a fixed outreach quota are more clinically actionable.
all_issues_adjudicated: Confounding the SDOH feature contribution with the model class difference (GBT vs. logistic regression) makes it impossible to attribute observed improvements to SDOH features specifically; a within-model-class feature ablation is required.

SDOH features have informative missingness — patients from underserved communities are most likely to have missing SDOH records, meaning simple imputation systematically underestimates the SDOH signal where it matters most.

Stratified random split over five years of clinical data ignores policy-driven distribution shifts (CMS readmission penalties, coding practice changes) that alter the feature-label relationship over time.
AUC-ROC as the primary metric ignores the operational threshold at which the intervention is deployed; calibration and performance at a fixed outreach quota are more clinically actionable.
